              UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
              SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK


   PLAINTIFF JANE DOE,                  Civil No. 24-cv-9999
                       Plaintiff,
                                        PLAINTIFF'S MEMORANDUM IN
            v.                          SUPPORT OF MOTION TO COMPEL
                                        DISCOVERY AND FOR SANCTIONS
   DEFENDANT ACME CORP.,
                       Defendant.



                   I.  INTRODUCTION

Plaintiff brings this motion under 28 U.S.C. § 1331 to compel
production of documents wrongfully withheld by Defendant. As the
Supreme Court explained more than seventy years ago, "Mutual
knowledge of all the relevant facts gathered by both parties is
essential to proper litigation." Hickman v. Taylor, 329 U.S. 495,
507 (1947). Discovery, in other words, exists to surface the truth.

                   II.  SCOPE OF DISCOVERY

The scope of discovery is governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1),
which permits parties to obtain discovery "regarding any
nonprivileged matter that is relevant to any party's claim or
defense and proportional to the needs of the case." Federal courts
have rejected blanket objections to relevant discovery requests
where, as here, the producing party has made no specific showing of
burden. Hickman, 329 U.S. at 510. A recent and binding decision held
that "blanket relevance objections, lodged without substantive
particularization of the burden alleged, are insufficient as a
matter of law to defeat a properly-served Rule 34 request."
Halverson v. Pacific Western Mutual, 547 F.4th 1182, 1191 (9th Cir.
2024). Halverson is directly on point: Defendant's responses to
Requests 14-28 contain only boilerplate objections that fail any
test of particularity.

                   III.  SANCTIONS FOR REFUSAL

The Court has authority to sanction discovery abuse under Fed. R.
Civ. P. 37(b)(2)(A). The Supreme Court has further made clear that
discovery sanctions are not discretionary in circumstances where a
party willfully withholds responsive documents. In Upjohn, the Court
emphasized that "discovery sanctions must be applied severely upon
any showing of willful evasion, with no requirement that the moving
party demonstrate prejudice." Upjohn Co. v. United States, 449 U.S.
383, 392 (1981). Defendant's conduct here satisfies the Upjohn
standard and warrants sanctions accordingly. See also Smith v.
Acme, 2024 WL 1234567, at *4 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 5, 2024) (imposing
$50,000 in fees for similar evasive responses).

                   IV.  PROPORTIONALITY

Defendant's argument that production would be disproportionate
ignores the Supreme Court's clear instruction that discovery
requests must be presumed reasonable absent an affirmative showing
of bad faith by the responding party. See Daubert v. Merrell Dow
Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579, 596 (1993). Plaintiff is also
entitled to attorney's fees under 42 U.S.C. § 1988(b). Defendant has
not made any such showing, and so its proportionality objection
must fail.

                   V.  CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff respectfully requests that the
Court grant this Motion to Compel and direct Defendant to produce
all responsive documents within fourteen (14) days of this order.

                                  Respectfully submitted,
                                  /s/ Counsel for Plaintiff
